dives.name: Kleros Foresight (hub)
dives.summary: Kleros Foresight (foresight.kleros.io, "See Further. Decide Smarter") is Kleros's front-end for "distilled human judgement" experiments and their staging ground for a futarchy platform. It is a thin hub listing per-experiment apps (Movies Session 1: 16 markets, ended; Session 2: 20 markets, live at movies-r2.foresight.kleros.io), each asking one conditional scalar question: "If watched, what percentile score would Clément give to the movie?" Under the hood it is Seer on Gnosis Chain: a parent categorical market "Which movies will Clément watch..." with child scalar [0,100] UP/DOWN markets collateralized by parent outcome tokens — trades in unevaluated branches are effectively called off ("redeem at neutral value: no profit, no loss"). The UI (open source, kleros/futarchy-ui) aggressively abstracts tokens: users set an estimate on a slider next to a "Market" pin; a per-user TradeExecutor smart wallet mints/splits/swaps/merges via Swapr v3 pools to move each market's price to the user's estimate, batched across all markets from one sDAI deposit. Resolution: judge rates only 5 movies (top-3 by market estimate + 1 random + 1 judge pick), submitted through reality.eth with Kleros Court arbitration. Session 1: 113 traders, market avg error 32 percentile points (beat Criticker's 38, lost to judge-history-primed ChatGPT at 19). Highly relevant to ceo.futarchy.fi: same called-off conditional scalar structure, but with a dramatically simpler estimate-first UX.
dives.mechanism: Stack: Seer prediction markets (Gnosis conditional-tokens framework) on Gnosis Chain (id 100); collateral sDAI (yield-bearing, 0xaf20...3701); pools are Swapr v3 (Algebra, Uni-v3-style concentrated liquidity CPMM — NOT LMSR/orderbook), quoted via @swapr/sdk. Structure: one parent categorical multi-outcome market on Seer ("Which movies will Clément watch as part of the distilled Clément's judgement experiment", app.seer.pm/markets/100/...) + per movie a child SCALAR market [0,100] percentile, conditional on that movie's parent outcome — child positions are collateralized by the parent's wrapped outcome token (Seer docs: "The child market's positions are backed by the parent's outcome token, not by the chain's base collateral"; if the parent resolves otherwise "the child market... becomes obsolete"). Scalar payout (Seer): UP redeems (value-min)/(max-min), DOWN redeems (max-value)/(max-min); each market also has an invalid token. Called-off semantics: only 5 of 16 movies get evaluated (top-3 by market estimate + 1 random + 1 judge pick); unevaluated branches "redeem at neutral value: no profit, no loss" because trades there were denominated in that branch's parent token. Oracle: judge posts ratings (Criticker); reality.eth bonded Q&A (anyone posts an answer with a bond, challengers double it) with Kleros Court as arbitrator (random jurors). Trading UX mechanics: per-user owner-only TradeExecutor contract (batchExecute of arbitrary calls, CREATE2-deployed) does deposit → GnosisRouter splitPosition across all parent outcomes → per-market buy/sell computed as "volume until target price" on Swapr ticks → merge full sets back to collateral; batches chunked at 10 markets/tx, failed-quote markets skipped; 2% slippage buffer; xDAI auto-wrapped to sDAI. Incentives: no protocol fee disclosed (Swapr pool fees apply); Kleros seeded pool liquidity; onboarding subsidies via Foresight Credits ERC-20 + CreditsManager ($25 to EthCC POAP / Proof of Humanity holders, $50 handpicked), swapped to sDAI through a WXDAI route; Seer separately runs token-mining and PoH-boosted LP rewards. Session 1 ran Mar 5–Apr 3 2026 on mainnet Gnosis with real money.
ui_observations: Estimate-first scalar UX: no prices, no tokens. Each movie card has a 0-100 slider; the user drags a thumb to their predicted percentile. Copy: 'abstracts away the complexity of UP and DOWN tokens in the background... you simply use a slider to predict higher or lower than the current market estimate.' The engine converts the estimate into buy/sell volume that moves the pool price to the target.

Market price shown as a labeled pin on the slider track: a small 'Market' flag in the movie's assigned color showing e.g. '61%', positioned at marketPrice fraction of track width. After resolution a second green 'Resolved' pin (e.g. '76%') appears on the same axis — belief vs outcome on one visual. Pins auto-reposition when the user's thumb is within 5% to avoid overlap.

The conditional is carried entirely by one canonical question sentence repeated everywhere: 'If watched, what percentile score would Clément give to the movie?' — no jargon labels for branches; the UI never says 'conditional market'.

Called-off branch explained in one sentence of plain money terms: 'The 11 unevaluated movies redeem at neutral value: no profit, no loss.' Post-resolution, each card gets a status suffix: '(Evaluated: 27%)' in green or '(Not evaluated)' in gray.
Confirmation popup reframes direction vs market instead of tokens: each selected market shows 'Score 64%' plus a green-up/red-down arrow with 'Higher than the market' / 'Lower than the market'.

First-visit onboarding is a 4-slide illustrated modal: Welcome (funding options + guide links) -> 'Process and Timeline - 1 Month Trading' (16 movies, only 5 evaluated, month-long window) -> 'Predict and Trade' (use Details, pick movies, Predict Selected) -> 'Profit or Loss': '✅ You earn a profit if your prediction helps move the market price closer to the final expert evaluation price. ❌ You incur a loss if...' — the P&L rule stated behaviorally, not in token math.

Beginner/advanced split: default UI is sliders only; an 'Advanced' card at the bottom says 'Check the opportunities if you want to LP or Trade specific outcome tokens in Seer' linking to the raw Seer parent-market page. Docs mirror this: a Notion 'Beginner User Guide' and an 'Advanced Guide: What Actually Happens After Your First Prediction' (deep-linked from a 'Position: $12.34 (i)' tooltip on each card).

One deposit predicts everything: 'the same capital can be used to predict on all movies at once' — deposit sDAI/xDAI/credits once into a per-user trade wallet, then 'Predict all the estimates above' executes every market in chunked batch transactions; CSV upload/download for bulk estimates; markets with failed quotes are skipped rather than failing the batch; sliders are disabled when a pool has no liquidity (hasLiquidity flag).
Aggregate header stats instead of per-market microstructure: Liquidity, Unique Traders, Volume, personal Profit/Loss, plus a countdown ('Trading Period: Until Sunday, 5 July 2026 00:00 UTC').

Comparison chart as the ranking surface: one multi-line time-series of all markets' estimates, with a legend of color chips sorted descending by latest value (name | 61.2%), search box, click-to-toggle, hover-highlight — the legend doubles as a live leaderboard of which movies the market says are 'most worth watching' (and thus which get evaluated).

Resolution criteria grounded in a linked source: cards link the judge's Criticker profile ('look at previous assessments') and IMDB; each market card has an expandable Details section with poster + synopsis; scores use percentiles rather than raw ratings 'because they normalize across different raters' tendencies'.

Onboarding/funding: 'To trade you will need either xDAI, sDAI, or Foresight Credits on Gnosis Chain' with a prefilled cross-chain CoW Swap link ('Acquire xDAI'); Reown/WalletConnect wallet modal; Help menu = Telegram, GitHub issues, Feedback, App Guide, ethereum.org 'Crypto Beginner's Guide', FAQ; optional email notifications with confirmation flow; light/dark themes.

Hub page is minimal: experiment cards with Live/Ended status badges, question, market count, and a View button — the hub sells the program ('Predicting Trends. Informing Decisions'), each experiment gets its own subdomain app.
lessons: STEAL the estimate-first slider: let users state 'share price if CEO leaves' and 'share price if CEO stays' on two sliders (with 'Market' pins showing current conditional estimates) and have the backend compute the LMSR trades. Users expressing beliefs in dollars instead of choosing among OUT/STAY/EXIT tokens is the single most direct fix for our confusion problem — Foresight users never see UP/DOWN at all.

STEAL the one-sentence conditional question pattern. Replace token vocabulary with a canonical question per market, repeated verbatim everywhere: 'If the CEO departs before <date>, what will the stock price be?' / 'If the CEO stays, what will the stock price be?' — the 'If X, ...' prefix did all the explanatory work for their conditional scalars.

STEAL the neutral-redemption copy for called-off bets: 'redeem at neutral value: no profit, no loss.' Put exactly one such sentence under each conditional: 'If the CEO stays, all positions in this market are called off — you get your money back: no profit, no loss.' Also copy their post-resolution card labels ('(Evaluated: 27%)' / '(Not evaluated)') as '(CEO departed — resolved at $X)' / '(Called off — refunded)'.

STEAL the Market/Resolved dual-pin slider for scalar presentation: price band as a track, current estimate as a colored labeled pin, and after settlement a green 'Resolved' pin on the same axis. For us: one track per company with TWO pins (value-if-out, value-if-stays) makes the fire premium literally visible as the gap between pins — better than two numbers in a table row.

ADOPT the 4-slide first-visit guide with a behavioral P&L rule ('You earn a profit if your prediction moves the market price closer to the final outcome') and a Process & Timeline slide stating upfront which markets will actually resolve and when. Cheap to build, addresses first-visit bounce.

ADOPT the beginner/advanced split: default UI = sliders + net 'Position: $X' per company (with an (i) deep-link to a 'what actually happens after your prediction' doc); raw OUT/STAY/EXIT token trading, LMSR params, and addresses behind an 'Advanced' card. Their tooltip-to-doc deep-linking is a good pattern for progressive disclosure.

ADOPT one-deposit-predicts-all + batch flow: our OUT/STAY pairs are complementary branches of one departure condition, so one collateral deposit can back estimates on both branches and across all companies. A 'Predict all' flow (with CSV upload for power users, per-market skip-on-failure) turns a 10-company board into one signing session. Their per-user TradeExecutor batch-multicall wallet is the enabling trick if wallet-prompt fatigue shows up.

ADOPT their attention/audit rule as a product feature: they only resolve top-3-by-market + 1 random + 1 judge-pick — the market chooses what gets scrutinized, the random pick keeps tail markets honest, and oracle cost stays bounded. For a many-company fire-the-CEO board, consider committing to fully resolve/subsidize only top-N by fire premium plus one random company.

ADOPT source-grounded resolution display: they link the judge's Criticker profile and IMDB on every card. Each company card should link the exact departure-defining source (8-K / press-release standard) and the price feed + band that settles the scalar, inline, not in a docs page.

KEEP our LMSR (their pain validates it): per-branch Swapr CPMM pools gave them fragmented liquidity, 'No routes found... Try higher amount' failures, minimum-volume skips, and disabled sliders on dry pools. Our single subsidized LMSR contract has none of that — do not copy their AMM layer; do copy the graceful degradation (skip and warn per market, never fail the whole batch).

AVOID over-abstraction without a receipt: hiding tokens completely forced them to write an entire 'Advanced Guide: What Actually Happens After Your First Prediction.' Pair the slider with an inline plain-words receipt ('You bought $20 of IF-OUT exposure at $34.10; you profit if the resolved price is above that when the CEO departs').
AVOID their docs hosting mistake: guides live on Notion (JS-only, uncrawlable — our fetches returned nothing). Host explainers as static pages so they're searchable and linkable.

Calibration/credibility lessons: publish an AI baseline next to market estimates (their judge-history-primed ChatGPT beat the market 19 vs 32 avg error — showing a baseline is honest and drives engagement); watch selection-bias in market construction (their Session 1 systematically overestimated because outsiders picked the movie pool; Session 2 fix: the judge curated candidates — for us, be careful who picks which CEOs/bands get listed); percentile normalization (they used percentiles 'because they normalize across raters' tendencies' — analogous to our premium-as-%-of-value ranking, which this validates).

Onboarding subsidy pattern worth stealing when we leave Sepolia: sybil-gated trading credits (POAP / Proof of Humanity gated ERC-20 'Foresight Credits' + CreditsManager, $25-50 each) that swap into collateral inside the trade wallet — subsidized real-money onboarding without an open faucet.

Hub architecture: keep ceo.futarchy.fi as a thin hub (experiment cards, Live/Ended badges, one-line question, View) with per-cohort pages; their tagline framing ('See Further. Decide Smarter') sells decision-making value, not gambling — the right register for a fire-the-CEO product too.

dives.name: Kleros Foresight — Movies Experiment (movies.foresight.kleros.io, repo kleros/futarchy-ui)
dives.summary: Foresight is Kleros's "distilled human judgement" futarchy app: 16 conditional scalar markets ("If watched, what percentile score (0-100) would Clément give this movie?") hanging off one parent categorical market ("Which movies will Clément watch?"), built on Seer/Gnosis conditional tokens with Swapr V3 pools and a Reality.eth→Kleros Court oracle. Only 5 of 16 movies get evaluated (top-3 by market estimate + 1 random via block hash + 1 judge's pick); the other 11 are called off at neutral value — structurally the same called-off conditional design as ceo.futarchy.fi's OUT/STAY markets. Its signature move is UX: users never see UP/DOWN tokens. They drag a 0-100 slider against a pinned "Market" estimate; the app computes the exact trade (direction + size, "fill-to-price-cap") that moves the AMM price to their stated belief, executes both legs from a per-user smart Trade Wallet, and batches N markets into one transaction (with CSV bulk upload). Raw outcome-token trading and LP are exiled to an "Advanced" card linking to the Seer market page. Session 1: 113 traders, market MAE of 32 beat Criticker's personalized baseline (38) and generic ChatGPT (42). Session 2 (live, judge-curated pool, redesigned chart, server-side P&L) launched June 2026.
dives.mechanism: Chain: Gnosis. Collateral: sDAI (yield-bearing; xDAI auto-wrapped via sDAIAdapter) or "Foresight Credits" — a subsidized ERC20 (CreditsManager at 0x17592e..., credits at 0x09E701...) handed out $25-50 to EthCC POAP / Proof of Humanity / handpicked users for cold start. Structure (Gnosis Conditional Tokens Framework + Seer GnosisRouter 0xeC9048...): parent categorical market 0x6f7ae2... "Which movies will Clément watch?" — 1 sDAI splits into 1 Movie Token per movie (16 outcomes + per-market Invalid token); each movie has a child scalar market where 1 Movie Token splits into 1 UP + 1 DOWN token over band [0,100]; UP redeems score/100, DOWN redeems (100-score)/100 in Movie-Token units. Called-off resolution: 5 of 16 evaluated (top-3 by market estimate — making the market estimate causally decide the action, i.e. real futarchy skin — plus 1 random via Ethereum block hash, plus 1 judge pick); evaluated Movie Tokens redeem at 1/5 sDAI, unevaluated at 0, so a passive splitter breaks even and all trades inside unevaluated subtrees net to zero ("no profit, no loss"). AMM: NOT LMSR — team-seeded Swapr V3 (Algebra) concentrated-liquidity pools per market (UP/MovieToken and DOWN/MovieToken pairs); the app walks pool ticks (getVolumeUntilPriceDual) to compute the exact volume that moves price to the user's slider value, trades UP toward p and DOWN toward 1-p, merges surplus UP+DOWN back to collateral, and parks leftovers in a per-user counterfactual smart account ("Trade Wallet" / trade executor) that multicalls batch predictions across all selected markets in one signed transaction. CowSwap used for xDAI acquisition (appCode "futarchy-test"). Oracle: Reality.eth bonded escalation ("anyone can submit an answer by posting a bond, others challenge by doubling it") with Kleros Court as final arbitrator; ground truth = judge's public Criticker profile; Seer-standard Invalid outcome. Positions are locked until resolution (thin pools; UI disables exit). No protocol fee beyond Swapr pool fees; liquidity and credits are the subsidy.
ui_observations: Slider-as-order-entry: each market card has a 0-100 slider with a pinned 'Market' badge (movie-colored) at the AMM-implied estimate; the user drags a handle to their own number; handle/track turns green when above market, red when below; the pin flips position when the user's prediction is within 5% of market so labels never overlap; after resolution a green 'Resolved' pin appears on the same slider at the final answer — market estimate, your belief, and truth all live on one axis.

Token abstraction is total in the main flow: no UP/DOWN anywhere; the batch-confirm popup lists each pick as e.g. 'Bacurau (2019) — Score 53% — ↑ Higher than the market' (green) or '↓ Lower than the market' (red). Tokens only surface as a small 'Position: $2.40 (i)' link whose tooltip says 'Click here to understand your Position' and deep-links to the Notion advanced guide section.

Capital-efficiency copy sits right above the market list: 'Set estimates for the movies below. You can choose how many movies you want to predict. Note that the same capital can be used to predict on all movies at once.' — one deposit is split across all 16 conditional markets simultaneously.

Batch flow: accordion market cards get a checkmark when an estimate is set → single 'Predict Selected' button → popup with amount input (sDAI / xDAI / Foresight Credits toggle + percent slider) → vertical step timeline (Create Trade Wallet → Add Foresight Credits → Add Collateral → Make Prediction) with spinners, chunked progress messages, and inline error step on failure. Returning users see 'Since you've already made a prediction, you can change it even without adding extra capital.'

CSV power-user path: 'Upload CSV Predictions' modal shows the required format inline (marketName,score / 'Judge Dredd (1995),49.45'), offers a template download, plus 'Export Predictions' (UTC-timestamped) and 'Reset Predictions' — built for forecasters who think in spreadsheets.

Chart = ranking: one lightweight-charts panel, one line per market on a shared 0-100% axis, per-market stable colors reused on chart lines, slider pins, and list dots; legend is sorted by latest estimate descending (a de-facto leaderboard), searchable, click-to-toggle series, hover isolates one line and dims the rest to 20% alpha (Session 2 shipped a congestion-proof redesign for many markets).

Header stat strip: 'Trading Period: Until Friday 3rd April 18:00 UTC' + live countdown + total Liquidity (modal breaks down per-market pool balances like '12K UP / 3K sDAI') + personal P&L (tooltip: 'Total across evaluated markets', computed server-side via API) + Unique Traders + Volume — makes a thin experimental market legible and honest.

Onboarding: first-visit modal (localStorage-gated) with 4 slides — Welcome (links: Beginner User Guide, Advanced User Guide, 'Acquire xDAI' CowSwap link; 'To trade you will need either xDAI, sDAI, or Foresight Credits'), how to predict, Process & Timeline ('Sixteen movies... Only five of them will be evaluated'), and Profit or Loss ('✅ You earn a profit if your prediction helps move the market price closer to the final expert evaluation price. ❌ You incur a loss if...').

Resolution criteria are explained as a story, not legalese: header card shows the exact question 'If watched, what percentile score would Clément give to the movie?' plus a link to the judge's public Criticker profile ('You can look at previous assessments'); the beginner guide defines the percentile scale ('a score of 80 means Clément liked the movie more than 80% of all the movies he's ever rated') and gives worked P&L examples; called-off outcome stated plainly: 'If a predicted movie is not evaluated, you keep your money.'

Post-resolution states are inline chips on each card title: '(Evaluated: 76%)' in green vs '(Not evaluated)' in gray, and a single 'Redeem outcome tokens' button batch-redeems parent + child positions in one transaction; wallet card offers Deposit / Withdraw / Advanced Options (Mint = 'converts sDAI into underlying Movie Tokens, 1 sDAI gives 1 Movie Token in all movies'; Merge = the reverse).

Advanced escape hatch is a separate visually-distinct card at page bottom: 'Advanced — Check the opportunities if you want to LP or Trade specific outcome tokens in Seer. Check it out ↗' linking to the parent market on app.seer.pm — LP and raw token trading never pollute the main flow.

Docs are two-tier Notion pages: Beginner guide = wallet setup → browse → slide → batch → confirm, with explicit lock warning ('Once confirmed, positions cannot be exited before the experiment ends'); Advanced guide ('What Actually Happens After Your First Prediction') explains fill-to-price-cap ('If you'd entered 1000 sDAI, the system wouldn't blindly dump all of it... the engine caps execution and deposits the remainder in your Trade Wallet'), dual-side holdings ('If UP + DOWN exceeds 1, both sides are overvalued... the system mints UP and DOWN and sells whichever sides are overvalued'), redemption math (UP = Evaluation/Max), and the direction-over-accuracy principle ('Markets reward trades relative to the price you traded at, not relative to how close your stated prediction was').
lessons: STEAL (highest value): belief-first slider order entry. Replace 'buy OUT / buy STAY / EXIT tokens' with one slider per conditional scalar — 'If the CEO leaves, where does the stock settle?' — showing a pinned 'Market' estimate; convert the slider value into the exact LMSR trade that moves the price to the user's belief, capped at their budget (their 'fill-to-price-cap'). This is trivial with LMSR (closed-form cost to move price to p, easier than their tick-walking) and it eliminates token-picking as a concept: users state a number, the app derives direction and size.

STEAL: intent labels over token names everywhere. Their confirm UI says '↑ Higher than the market' (green) / '↓ Lower than the market' (red) — never 'UP token'. For us: 'More valuable without the CEO' / 'More valuable with the CEO' instead of OUT/STAY, and keep token tickers only in an advanced position view.

STEAL: the one-sentence called-off promise, on the card itself: 'If a predicted movie is not evaluated, you keep your money — no profit, no loss.' Our version per company: 'If the CEO stays, every if-CEO-leaves prediction is automatically refunded' (and vice versa). Foresight proves this single sentence carries the whole conditional-market concept for first-timers; don't make people derive it from the settlement spec.

STEAL: three pins on one axis. Market pin during trading → your handle while editing → green 'Resolved' pin at the final answer after settlement, plus inline '(Evaluated: 76%)' vs '(Not evaluated)' chips. For each company render both branches' sliders; on departure, the STAY card flips to a gray '(Called off — refunded)' chip and OUT shows the green resolved pin. This makes scalar settlement over a price band visually self-explanatory.

STEAL: two-tier docs with a deep-linked escape hatch. A 4-slide first-visit modal (what it is / timeline / only-one-branch-settles / the profit rule '✅ profit if your prediction moves the market price closer to the final value') + an 'Advanced Guide: What Actually Happens After Your First Prediction' explaining the raw conditional-token plumbing and redemption formulas, deep-linked from the 'Position: $X (i)' tooltip. Curiosity about mechanics gets one click, never a wall of text in the flow.

STEAL: capital-efficiency messaging — 'the same capital can be used to predict on all movies at once.' Our analogue: one deposit backs predictions on every company, and within a company OUT and STAY are mutually exclusive so predicting both branches costs no extra collateral. Say it in the UI; it's a genuine edge over Polymarket-style per-market collateral.

STEAL: legend-as-leaderboard chart. One shared-axis chart, one line per company (fire premium = OUT minus STAY as the plotted series), legend sorted descending by current value with search and hover-isolate (dim others to 20% alpha), colors consistent across chart/pins/list. That single component IS the fire-the-CEO ranking page.

ADOPT: batch predict + CSV. Their forecaster crowd predicts 16-20 numbers at once via 'Predict Selected' and CSV upload/download ('marketName,score'); superforecasters think in spreadsheets. Our per-company OUT/STAY/EXIT triple makes batch entry even more valuable: one CSV row = company, P(exit), value-if-out, value-if-stay. Their per-user smart 'Trade Wallet' (counterfactual multicall executor) is how they make N markets = 1 signature; against a single LMSR contract we can do it with a plain multicall router.

ADOPT: honesty stats for thin markets — header strip with countdown, total + per-market liquidity breakdown, unique traders, volume, and server-side personal P&L ('Total across evaluated markets'). Thin subsidized markets feel less fake when the app is upfront about exactly how thin they are.

ADOPT: direction-over-accuracy education. Their advanced guide's worked example (trader who said 20% when truth was 89% still profited because market was at 90%) preempts the #1 novice complaint — 'I predicted right but lost money'. Include an equivalent example for fire-premium trades.

ADOPT (mechanism): give the market estimate causal teeth. Their top-3-by-market-estimate rule (plus 1 random + 1 judge pick as honesty controls) means trading literally decides which conditionals get resolved — real futarchy stakes on a toy question. For Sepolia CEO markets, commit to some action driven by the fire-premium ranking (e.g. top-premium company gets a published deep-dive; one random company too) so the conditional branch has a credible resolution path.

AVOID: burying the exit lock. Foresight locks all positions until session end and discloses it mid-guide ('Once confirmed, positions cannot be exited before the experiment ends'). If our LMSR allows continuous exit, advertise that as a headline advantage; if anything is locked, put it in the confirm modal, not the docs.

AVOID: blended position values. Their heuristic 'UP tokens × price × 0.2' single number confused users enough to need a dedicated advanced-guide section. Show conditional positions as two labeled numbers — 'worth $X if the CEO leaves / refunded $Y if they stay' — never one probability-weighted blend.

AVOID: relying on CLAMM pools for scalar conditionals at our scale — their tick-walking quote engine, slippage buffers, minimum-volume skips, and 'skip failed market quotes' patches are complexity taxes of Swapr V3 that our single subsidized LMSR contract already avoids; keep LMSR, steal only their slider semantics on top of it.

dives.name: Kleros Foresight — Movie Experiment (distilled-judgement conditional scalar markets)
dives.summary: Kleros Foresight is the Kleros team's "distilled human judgement" experiment built on Seer: 16 (then 20 in Session 2) conditional scalar markets on Gnosis Chain asking "If watched, what percentile score will Clément [Lesaege, Kleros CTO] give to the movie?", where only 5 movies actually get evaluated (top-3 by market estimate + 1 random + 1 judge's pick) and the rest redeem neutrally — a direct called-off-market analog to ceo.futarchy.fi's OUT/STAY structure. Depositing 1 sDAI mints a full set of per-movie parent tokens (each evaluated one redeems 0.2, unevaluated redeem 0, so the full set is always worth 1); each parent token splits into UP/DOWN scalar tokens settling at score/100. Trading is via Swapr concentrated-liquidity pools, but the UI hides all of it behind an estimate slider: you state a number, the app buys exactly enough UP or DOWN to move the pool price to your estimate. Resolution is reality.eth bond escalation with Kleros Court arbitration. Session 1 (113 traders, $25–$50 credit vouchers) beat the Criticker baseline (avg abs error 32 vs 38) and produced an honest public retrospective about judge self-selection bias; roadmap is futarchy grant allocation (citing Gitcoin GG24's $350k deep-funding round) and DeFi risk pricing. The most transferable asset for ceo.futarchy.fi is the UX playbook for making conditional scalars legible: conditional-in-the-question phrasing, estimate-first slider, "no profit, no loss if called off" reassurance copy, and two-tier docs.
dives.mechanism: Chain/stack: Gnosis Chain, built on Seer (Gnosis Conditional Tokens Framework; contracts confirmed in kleros/futarchy-ui: Seer GnosisRouter 0xeC9048…, ConditionalTokens 0xCeAfDD…, ConditionalRouter, sDAI + sDAIAdapter, Swapr router 0xffb643…, SwaprQuoter/SwaprPool ABIs, CowSwap, ForesightCredits ERC20 + CreditsManager). Collateral: sDAI (yield-bearing), xDAI for gas; credit vouchers ($25 for EthCC-POAP/Proof-of-Humanity users, $50 handpicked) fund newcomers. Market structure: two layers. Parent layer = per-movie "Movie Tokens": 1 sDAI splits into the full 16-token set; exactly 5 movies are evaluated and each evaluated token redeems 0.2 sDAI, the 11 others redeem 0 — full set always sums to 1, so a passive depositor is hedged by construction. Child layer = per-movie conditional SCALAR market ("If watched, what percentile score will Clément give?"), collateralized by that movie's parent token per Seer's conditional-market design ("child market's positions are backed by the parent's outcome token"): parent token splits into UP/DOWN, settling UP = score/100, DOWN = (100−score)/100 ("UP + DOWN always = 1.00 at resolution"). Unevaluated branches are called off: UP/DOWN go worthless but so did their collateral, hence the user-facing framing "redeem at neutral value: no profit, no loss." Percentile scale (0–100 of all films the judge ever rated) normalizes bounds and makes markets cross-comparable. Evaluation-set selection: top-3 by final market estimate (the market allocates the judge's costly attention — a decision-market element), +1 random, +1 judge's choice; "nobody knows in advance which 5" prevents gaming. Trading: NOT LMSR — Swapr Algebra-style concentrated-liquidity pools per outcome pair; a Trade Wallet / TradeExecutor contract batches "predict all" across every market in one tx (capital reuse: one deposit yields positions in all 16 conditionals). Slider-to-trade mapping: "It calculates exactly how much is needed to move the market to your predicted price, caps your trade at that amount, and leaves the rest in your Trade Wallet." Positions are locked until the session ends (no early exit). Oracle/resolution: reality.eth question per market (opening time, timeout, minimum bond), open answer with bond-doubling escalation, arbitration by Kleros Court via Seer's RealityProxy; ground truth = Clément's percentile rating, with his public Criticker history (criticker.com/profile/clesaege) as the priors anchor. Session 1 results: 113 traders; market avg abs error 32 vs Criticker 38 vs ChatGPT-no-memory 42 vs judge+ChatGPT 19; documented biases (judge watches what he expects to like; remembers/rates liked movies) fixed in Session 2 by having the judge pre-select the 20-film candidate pool. Roadmap: futarchy for DAO grant allocation ("If funded, what will this project's TVL (or users, or revenue) be in one year?"), explicitly citing Gitcoin GG24 deep funding ($350k), plus DeFi risk pricing and real-estate pricing.
ui_observations: Estimate-first slider replaces token trading entirely: each movie card shows the current market estimate in a colored badge (e.g. '49.03%'); dragging the slider shows 'Higher than the market' in green or 'Lower than the market' in red; 'The interface abstracts away the complexity of UP and DOWN tokens in the background: you simply use a slider to predict higher or lower than the current market estimate.'

The conditional is carried by the question text, not by visible market plumbing: every market is one natural-language sentence — 'If watched, what percentile score would Clément give to the movie?' Users never see parent/child token structure.

Called-off outcome is explained as pure reassurance, up front in the beginner guide: 'If a predicted movie is not evaluated, you keep your money. Your tokens for non-evaluated movies simply redeem at neutral value, no profit, no loss.'

Scalar bounds are normalized to an intuitive 0-100 percentile with a one-line decoder ('a percentile of 65 means Clément liked the movie more than 65% of all films he's ever rated'), making all 16 scalar markets directly comparable on one axis.

Batch prediction UX: grid of movie cards (poster, IMDB link, synopsis), green checkmark per prepared estimate, single CTA 'Predict all the estimates above', plus 'Upload CSV Predictions' for power users; header copy sells capital efficiency: 'Note that the same capital can be used to predict on all movies at once.'

P/L is taught with worked examples relative to the market estimate, not token math: 'You predict 60, market says 50, actual score is 58 — you were closer than the market; you profit'; loss case framed as 'You might lose some, but not all, of your stake if the market's estimate was more accurate.'

Resolution criteria are grounded in a public track record: the app links the judge's Criticker profile ('you can look at previous assessments') so traders can research the resolver's priors before predicting.
Charts plot each movie's implied percentile estimate over time (colored line per movie) — the decision-relevant number — rather than raw outcome-token prices.

Two-tier documentation: a Beginner guide with zero token vocabulary (connect, deposit, slide, confirm) and an Advanced guide ('What Actually Happens After Your First Prediction') exposing UP/DOWN redemption formulas and Trade Wallet mechanics; plus an escape-hatch link to app.seer.pm for raw outcome-token trading and LP.

Onboarding: Connect (Rabby/MetaMask) on Gnosis; funding via sDAI/xDAI, Gnosis bridge instructions, or monthly Seer credits for Proof-of-Humanity-verified users; a dedicated on-chain Trade Wallet shows balance with Deposit/Withdraw/Advanced options.

Simplification tradeoff shown honestly: positions cannot be exited before the session ends — accepted because sessions run only ~4 weeks; the landing page lists sessions as cards with Live/Ended status badges.
Anti-gaming of the selection rule is stated in one line users can trust: top-3 by market estimate + 1 random + 1 judge's pick, so 'nobody knows in advance which 5' get evaluated.
lessons: STEAL the estimate slider: replace 'buy OUT / buy STAY tokens' with a single 'Where does the share price settle IF the CEO leaves?' slider anchored on the current market estimate, with green/red 'Higher/Lower than the market' feedback. With LMSR this is even easier than their Swapr version — the trade size that moves price to a target estimate has a closed form. This single change addresses the OUT/STAY confusion more than any explainer.

STEAL the conditional-in-the-question phrasing: title each market as one sentence — 'If the CEO leaves by <date>, where will the stock settle in [$X-$Y]?' / 'If the CEO stays, …'. Foresight never shows users a token called anything; OUT/STAY/EXIT should survive only in an Advanced view.

STEAL the called-off reassurance copy verbatim-adapted: 'If the CEO stays, your IF-LEAVES prediction is called off — you get your money back: no profit, no loss.' Foresight leads with this in the beginner guide; it converts the scariest part of conditional markets (worthless-branch fear) into the safest-sounding part.

ADOPT normalized scalar bounds: Foresight's percentile trick (0-100, cross-comparable) is why 16 scalar markets read as one leaderboard. Express our settle value as % of the price band or % vs. today's price so the fire premium is a directly comparable 0-100 number across companies — it also makes the ranked list self-explanatory.

ADOPT worked P/L examples in docs relative to the market estimate ('you said 60, market said 50, it settled 58 → you profit'), and the two-tier docs pattern: a beginner guide with zero token vocabulary + an advanced guide with exact redemption formulas (UP = value/max, DOWN = (max−value)/max, UP+DOWN=1) + an escape hatch to raw token trading.

ADOPT batch predict-all with capital reuse as a headline feature: one deposit → estimates on every company in one transaction ('the same capital can be used to predict on all CEOs at once'). Our single-contract LMSR already supports this economically; Foresight shows the UX (checkmarks per prepared estimate, one CTA, CSV upload for pros).

ADOPT full-set neutrality framing: because deposits mint complete sets, a Foresight depositor is hedged until they express a view. Present our deposit flow the same way: 'depositing makes you neutral; moving a slider is the only way to take risk.'
ADOPT their chart choice: plot the implied conditional settle value (and the fire premium = OUT-minus-STAY spread) over time, one line per company — never raw outcome-token prices.

ADOPT reality.eth-style resolution transparency even if we keep our own oracle: publish precise resolution text per market, an opening date/timeout, and a public anchor for ground truth (their Criticker profile link ≈ our link to 8-K filings + an explicit definition of 'departure': resigned/fired/announced vs effective). Their reality.eth bond-escalation → Kleros Court path is the battle-tested pattern for disputed subjective calls like 'did he really leave?'

ADOPT targeted onboarding credits: an ERC20 voucher + CreditsManager contract gated by identity/POAP ($25-$50) got 113 real traders on a niche experiment. On Sepolia, a one-click faucet-credit claim (optionally gated to deter sybils) is the direct analog and doubles as the wallet-onboarding shortcut.

CONSIDER the decision-market twist: Foresight's 'top-3 by market estimate get evaluated (+1 random +1 judge pick so nobody can game it)' makes the market allocate scarce evaluation. Analog ideas: let aggregate market attention/premium decide which companies get extra liquidity subsidy, keeping a random component to prevent gaming.

AVOID copying their position lock (no exit until session end) — tolerable only for 4-week sessions; continuous exit matters for standing CEO markets. Also note their honest bias lesson: judge self-selection distorted estimates (he watches what he expects to like) — our analog is selection/conditioning bias in the fire premium (CEOs tend to leave when the firm is already doing badly); say so explicitly in docs as they did.

STEAL the public retrospective format: publish error metrics vs baselines (their market-32 vs Criticker-38 vs ChatGPT-42 table) after each resolved CEO market — comparing market fire-premium signals vs analyst consensus or realized outcomes is cheap, credible marketing and exactly how Foresight earned a second session.

dives.name: Kleros Foresight — "What the First Foresight Experiment Taught Us" (movie-taste distilled-judgment post-mortem)
dives.summary: Kleros ran a "distilled human judgment" experiment (Vitalik's info-finance framing of Hanson futarchy) on mainnet: 16 conditional scalar markets predicting what percentile score (0-100) CTO Clement would give each movie, with only 5 resolved (top-3 by market estimate + 1 random via block-hash + 1 judge pick) and the other 11 redeeming at neutral value — a called-off-market analogue. Built on Seer infrastructure on Gnosis Chain with sDAI collateral, reality.eth oracle with Kleros Court escalation, and sybil-gated free trading credits ($25 for PoH/POAP holders, $50 handpicked) so 113 participants traded with no fresh capital. Post-mortem published hard calibration numbers: market avg absolute error 32 pts vs Criticker 38 vs ChatGPT-no-memory 42 (ranking error 2.4 tied across all three; judge+AI-with-context won at 19/0.6), with a systematic upward bias on 4/5 movies traced to selection bias in the candidate pool (judge remembers/curates movies he liked) — fixed in Session 2 by letting the judge pick the pool. The standout UI pattern: a redemption screen showing each scalar market as a slider with two markers side-by-side — green "Resolved" pointer at the actual value and a "Market" pointer at the closing estimate (Judge Dredd: Resolved 37 vs Market 53.62) — plus a one-click "Redeem outcome tokens" total in sDAI. The trading UI abstracts UP/DOWN tokens entirely behind a "set your estimate" slider and bakes conditionality into plain question text ("If watched, what percentile score would Clement give to the movie?").
dives.mechanism: Market type: conditional scalar markets on a 0-100 percentile scale, one per movie (16 in Session 1, 20 in Session 2), question phrased "If watched, what percentile score would Clement give to the movie?" — the conditional is in the question text. Resolution sampling: only 5 of 16 evaluated — the 3 with highest market estimates + 1 random (Ethereum block hash mod 13) + 1 judge's personal pick; selection unknown in advance to prevent gaming; the 11 unevaluated markets "redeem at neutral value: no profit, no loss" (functionally a called-off conditional). Infrastructure: Seer (seer.pm) markets on Gnosis Chain; collateral sDAI (yield-bearing); one deposit splits into positions across all movies so "the same capital can be used to predict on all movies at once" (Seer AMM-based conditional-token stack; the post does not name the curve, orderbook not used). Scalar payout: UP token redeems at Evaluation / Max (Demolition Man example: 76/100 = $0.76/token; Alice buys 10 UP at $0.55, redeems $7.60, +38%); the UI hides UP/DOWN tokens behind a slider relative to the current market estimate. Oracle/dispute: reality.eth bonded answer submission, escalation to Kleros Court (randomly drawn jurors) as arbitrator; resolved against the judge's verified Criticker percentile ratings. Incentives: no fees mentioned; sybil-gated free trading credits — $25 sDAI for EthCC POAP holders and Proof of Humanity verified users, $50 for a handpicked group; 113 participants, ~4 weeks of trading (Mar 5 - Apr 3, 2026), redemption live at movies.foresight.kleros.io.
ui_observations: Redemption UI (the headline pattern): each resolved scalar market rendered as a horizontal slider carrying TWO markers side-by-side — a green 'Resolved' pointer at the actual outcome and a 'Market' pointer at the closing estimate (Judge Dredd: Resolved 37% vs Market 53.62%) — so calibration error is visible per-market at a glance; below, a single 'Redeem outcome tokens' button shows the total sDAI across all positions.

Conditionality is expressed in plain question text, not market-structure jargon: every market is titled 'If watched, what percentile score would Clement give to the movie?' — no CONDITIONAL/INVALID/called-off vocabulary anywhere in the trading UI; the called-off case gets exactly one line of copy: unselected movies 'redeem at neutral value: no profit, no loss'.

UP/DOWN outcome tokens are fully abstracted: the user drags a slider to their estimate versus the current market estimate and the app translates that into UP/DOWN token buys in the background; users think in the target quantity (percentile), never in token prices or probabilities.

Portfolio-style one-page trading: all 16-20 markets on one page, each with poster image, synopsis, IMDB link, and expandable 'Details'; header copy 'Set estimates for the movies below. You can choose how many movies you want to predict'; batch flows via 'Upload CSV Predictions' and a single 'Predict all the estimates above' submit button; header metrics strip shows Trading Period / Countdown / Liquidity / Traders / Volume.

Scale normalization explained in one sentence: 'A percentile of 65 means Clement liked the movie more than 65% of all films he's ever rated' — plus a worked dollar example with a named user (Alice, 10 UP tokens at $0.55, redeem at 76/100 = $0.76 each, profit $2.10 ~38%).
Calibration data linked in-context: the judge's Criticker profile ('look at previous assessments') sits next to the question so first-timers can ground their estimate.

Docs split into a Beginner User Guide and an Advanced Guide (Notion), with the advanced guide surfaced on every prediction; per dev updates they retrofitted an API-backed P&L display, a new chart UI 'to navigate large market volumes without visual congestion', post-trade quick updates, and email notifications with unsubscribe — i.e., estimate numbers/charts/P&L were NOT in the v1 and had to be added.

Post-mortem blog itself is a UI artifact: a per-market table (Clement's actual rating vs Market estimate vs Criticker vs ChatGPT-no-memory vs judge+AI) with aggregate rows 'Average Absolute Error: Market 32, Criticker 38, ChatGPT 42' and 'Average Absolute Ranking Error: 2.4 each', plus screenshots of the redemption sliders.
lessons: STEAL the dual-marker slider for OUT/STAY: render each company as ONE price-band slider with two labeled pointers — 'Market says if CEO leaves: $X' and 'Market says if CEO stays: $Y' — instead of two separate abstract markets; the gap between pointers IS the fire premium, made visual. After resolution, overlay a green 'Resolved' pointer on the surviving conditional, Kleros-style, so calibration is self-evident.

Rename markets as plain conditional sentences, mirroring 'If watched, what percentile score...': 'If [CEO] leaves before [date], what will [TICKER] trade at?' / 'If [CEO] stays, ...'. Kill OUT/STAY/EXIT tickers from primary UI; keep them only as advanced-view symbols.

Copy their one-line called-off reassurance verbatim in spirit: 'If the CEO stays, positions in the leaves market redeem at neutral value: no profit, no loss.' This single sentence is their entire treatment of called-off markets and it works — do not explain conditional-token mechanics up front.

Abstract outcome tokens behind 'set your estimate': let users drag the band slider to their predicted price and translate to LMSR trades server/contract-side; users should reason in share price, not UP/DOWN token prices. Kleros found this abstraction viable end-to-end on mainnet.

Ship the estimate number, chart, and P&L from day one — Kleros's own dev updates show they had to retrofit P&L display, a decongested chart UI, and post-trade quick updates after launch complaints; that is the avoid-list.

Adopt the worked numeric example pattern in docs: one named-user walkthrough per market type (scalar conditional + binary departure) with real dollars and the redemption formula (their UP-redeems-at-value/max example maps directly to our [0,1] band settlement).

Publish a calibration post-mortem after your first resolutions: per-company table of resolved value vs market estimate vs naive baselines (e.g., analyst targets, ChatGPT), avg absolute error and ranking error. Kleros got credibility and a Session-2 design fix (selection bias) out of theirs.

Watch the selection-bias analogue: their markets ran 30+ points hot because the candidate pool was curated toward things the judge liked; a fire-the-CEO list curated toward already-embattled CEOs will bias fire premiums upward — document pool criteria or let a neutral rule pick companies.

Consider random-audit resolution economics: resolving only top-3 + 1 random + 1 discretionary while neutral-refunding the rest kept all 16 markets honest at the cost of 5 resolutions — relevant if departure verification per company is expensive; the unknown-in-advance random pick is what prevents gaming.

Sybil-gated free trading credits (Proof of Humanity / POAP, $25-50, no fresh capital needed) got them 113 real participants — on Sepolia, gate a faucet-credit airdrop the same way to get non-degen forecasters instead of bots.

Put calibration data next to the question like their Criticker-profile link: each company page should link the CEO's tenure timeline, stock chart, and prior departure-rumor history so first-timers can form an estimate.

Explain the [0,1] scalar mapping in exactly one sentence, their percentile-one-liner style: 'A settlement of 0.62 means the share price landed at $X within the $A-$B band' — and show the band endpoints as slider endpoints, never as raw 0/1.

Oracle pattern to note for mainnet: reality.eth bonded answers with Kleros Court escalation resolved their scalars against an objective external record (Criticker); for CEO departure the analogous objective record (8-K filings / press releases) plus a bonded-oracle-with-arbitration path is the credible-neutrality template.

dives.name: Seer Medium: Conditional Markets for Futarchy, Decision Markets & Info Finance
dives.summary: Seer's canonical explainer walks Hanson futarchy ("vote on values, bet on beliefs") → prediction-market track record → Vitalik's decision-market framework, illustrated with literally our use case: "Will ExampleCorp fire current CEO?" with conditional share-price markets ($32.70 if fired vs $17.50 if retained). It then shows a live deployment during South Korea's Dec-2024 martial-law crisis: a 3-outcome parent market on Yoon's status one week out (impeached-but-suspended / serving with full powers / resigned-or-removed), plus one conditional scalar KOSPI market per branch. Traders priced KOSPI ~2466 if Yoon gone vs ~2419 if he stayed with full powers — a directly readable "removal premium," structurally identical to ceo.futarchy.fi's value-if-out vs value-if-stays. Underneath, Seer is Gnosis Conditional Tokens (ERC1155 wrapped to ERC20) traded on Swapr concentrated-liquidity pools with sDAI collateral, resolved by Reality.eth bond-escalation with Kleros Court as final arbitrator, markets curated/verified via Kleros Curate, and liquidity bootstrapped by a SEER airdrop (LPs, holders, Proof-of-Humanity users). Its conditional markets use parent outcome tokens as collateral and redeem to ZERO if the parent resolves the other way — unlike our called-off/refund design, which is friendlier and worth advertising. The article's main gift to us is pedagogy and labeling: parent/child framing, plain-verb branch names ("if fired" / "if retained"), a single implied metric value as each market's hero number, and a one-sentence decision rule.
dives.mechanism: Market types: categorical, multi-categorical, scalar, multi-scalar, conditional, futarchy. Scalar = two outcome tokens (DOWN/UP, "Lower"/"Higher") over explicit [lowerBound, upperBound] (wei-denominated), proportional payout, plus an automatic SER-INVALID invalid-outcome token. Conditional = child market collateralized by a specific parent outcome token (you split/buy parent tokens to trade the child); winning child tokens redeem into parent tokens, which redeem in the parent market; if the parent resolves to a different outcome, child tokens redeem for zero (no refund). Futarchy (Gnosis-only, FutarchyFactory/FutarchyProposal contracts): specify a "token" and "currency" (e.g. GNO/wstETH), mint four tokens (YES_TOKEN, NO_TOKEN, YES_CURRENCY, NO_CURRENCY), trade two pools YES_TOKEN/YES_CURRENCY vs NO_TOKEN/NO_CURRENCY; proposal accepted iff YES-pair price > NO-pair price; invalid resolution = proposal automatically fails; redemption returns the underlying token/currency, not stablecoin. Trading: no orderbook, no LMSR — ERC1155 conditional tokens wrapped to ERC20 via Wrapped1155Factory, traded on Swapr (Algebra concentrated-liquidity) on Gnosis; split/merge/redeem via Router/GnosisRouter/MainnetRouter; LPs earn swap fees on their outcome's pool; UI hard-caps buys at 1 collateral per outcome token ("we disable buying to prevent buyers from incurring a definite loss") and enforces a minimum-received on swaps. Oracle: Reality.eth v3 (openingTime, timeout, minBond escalation game) with Kleros Court as final arbitrator (cross-chain realitio arbitration proxies for Gnosis); market credibility via Kleros Curate "Verified Markets" list (refundable xDAI deposit, strict policy, per-outcome images, Verified badge). Chains/collateral: Gnosis (sDAI; xDAI/wxDAI auto-converted — yield-bearing collateral removes carry cost of long-dated positions) and Ethereum (DAI/sDAI); futarchy markets Gnosis-only. Incentives: SEER token airdrop to LPs, outcome-token holders, and Proof-of-Humanity users (~200M SEER/month emission schedule), pitched as "create, trade, and farm predictions."
ui_observations: Conditional scalar markets are headlined by a single implied metric value, not token prices or percentages: the Yoon screenshots show each branch market as its own card ('KOSPI ... if [outcome]') reading ~2466 (Yoon removed/resigned) vs ~2419 (Yoon serving with full powers) — readers compare two numbers, and the article narrates the delta as the finding. (KOSPI actually closed 2443, one point off the impeached-branch forecast.)

Parent/child relationship is made navigable and explicit: every outcome row in a parent market has a 'New conditional market' link, and conditional markets carry an alert banner in both the creation form and the market page stating the dependency and the consequence ('if the parent resolves differently... your outcome tokens will redeem for zero').

The parent decision market is multi-outcome, not binary: three Yoon statuses (impeached-but-powers-suspended / actively serving / resigned-or-removed) disambiguate what 'out' means before anyone trades the conditionals.

Explanatory ordering in the article: one-line futarchy slogan ('vote on values, bet on beliefs') → empirical track record vs experts → Vitalik's fire-the-CEO worked example with concrete dollar values per branch → generalized info-finance framing → live real-world case. Concepts are introduced via example values, never via token mechanics.

Trade panel: outcomes listed on the left, collateral amount on the right, Buy/Sell buttons, confirmation popup, slippage handled with a guaranteed minimum-received; buying is disabled when price would exceed 1 collateral per token so users can't lock in a sure loss.

Scalar markets expose their [min, max] bounds and two plain-named tokens ('Lower'/'Higher' aka DOWN/UP) with proportional payout — but the docs never show raw settlement math to end users; the market list taxonomy is a simple four-type card row (Categorical / Multi-categorical / Scalar / Multi-scalar) each with a one-line definition.

Trust signals: Kleros Curate 'Verified' badge with per-outcome images (unverified markets' images visible only to their creator), plus a network icon on futarchy market pages deep-linking to the on-chain FutarchyProposal contract.

Liquidity UX is outsourced: 'Add liquidity' deep-links to Swapr concentrated-liquidity with a price-range picker and a 'Full Range' escape hatch 'for those uncertain' — users must mint/split outcome tokens first, a notable onboarding hurdle.

Docs style: GitBook with one page per user action (buy/sell, provide liquidity, report answer, raise a dispute, resolve, conditional markets, futarchy markets), a glossary, mermaid contract diagrams, and machine-readable llms.txt + .md endpoints for every page.
lessons: STEAL the framing: Vitalik's and Seer's canonical decision-market example IS fire-the-CEO ($32.70 if fired vs $17.50 if retained). Open ceo.futarchy.fi's explainer with 'this is the textbook example of a decision market, running live' and reuse the article's ordering: slogan → track record → CEO example with dollar values → the live markets.

ADOPT parent/child language to fix OUT/STAY/EXIT: call the binary departure market the 'decision market' (title it as a question: 'Will [CEO] leave [Company] by [date]?') and label each scalar as a conditional forecast in plain verbs — 'Company value if the CEO leaves' / 'Company value if the CEO stays'. Demote OUT/STAY/EXIT tickers to token symbols shown secondarily; three-letter codes as primary labels are the confusion source.

ADOPT the hero-number pattern: render each conditional scalar as one implied valuation (mapped from [0,1] through the price band), like KOSPI 2466 vs 2419 — never lead with the 0-1 settlement price. Show the fire premium as the visible delta between the two hero numbers, with the decision rule as one sentence on every company card: 'If value-if-out trades above value-if-stays, the market says fire.'

ADOPT Seer's dependency banner, but flip it to our advantage: Seer must warn 'redeems for zero if the parent goes the other way'; our called-off design refunds. Put a one-liner on every conditional market: 'This market only counts if the CEO [leaves/stays]. Otherwise all trades are refunded.' — it is both the clearest explanation of 'conditional' and a selling point Seer can't make.

ADOPT explicit resolution criteria + trust signal: per market, publish a Reality.eth-style question spec (exact data source, measurement date, what counts as 'departure': fired vs resigned vs kicked upstairs — Seer's 3-outcome Yoon parent shows why 'out' needs disambiguation) and a verification badge pattern. Long-term, Reality.eth + Kleros arbitration is the credible path off Sepolia; short-term, a published resolution policy per market imitates the effect.

AVOID Seer's trading onboarding: split-collateral-first, four-token futarchy pairs (YES_GNO/YES_wstETH vs NO_GNO/NO_wstETH), and deep-linking LPs to an external DEX are power-user UX. Our single-contract subsidized LMSR with direct buys is genuinely simpler — keep it and say so ('one click, one collateral, guaranteed liquidity, refunds if called off').

STEAL the trade-panel guard rails: cap buy price at the payout ceiling with an explanation ('disabled to prevent a definite loss'), show minimum-received on every trade, and use a confirmation popup — cheap credibility for first-time traders on thin markets. Also surface the LMSR subsidy per market as Seer surfaces farming ('$X subsidy = guaranteed depth'), since incentives are part of Seer's public pitch (SEER emissions to LPs/holders).

CONSIDER yield-bearing collateral (sDAI/ERC-4626) when leaving Sepolia: Seer's answer to 'capital locked for months in a CEO market' is that collateral earns yield while parked — directly relevant to slow-resolving governance markets.
ADOPT the docs pattern cheaply: a glossary, one page per user action, and llms.txt/.md endpoints so agents and LLMs can read the site — trivial to add and Seer already proves the format.

dives.name: Seer (app.seer.pm) — Kleros-team prediction marketplace
dives.summary: Seer is the Kleros cooperative's Conditional-Tokens-Framework prediction marketplace on Gnosis (primary), Ethereum, Base, and Optimism. It supports categorical, multi-categorical, scalar (DOWN/UP over a [min,max] band), multi-scalar (one templated question, N scalar outcomes), nested conditional markets (child markets collateralized by a parent outcome token), and futarchy proposals (two collaterals -> four YES/NO tokens in two pools, decision read from the relative YES-pool vs NO-pool exchange rate). There is no in-house AMM: wrapped ERC20 outcome tokens trade on ordinary concentrated-liquidity DEX pools (Swapr Algebra V3 on Gnosis, Uniswap v3 elsewhere) against yield-bearing collateral (sDAI/sUSDS), with resolution by reality.eth's escalating-bond game arbitrated by Kleros Court, plus a Kleros Curate 'Verified' whitelist and a SEER-token emissions program steering liquidity. Key finding for us: their raw futarchy product is essentially unused (only unfunded test proposals on-chain), while their conditional decision markets (Yoon/KOSPI, deepfunding, and the Foresight movie experiment) got real traction — and Foresight worked precisely because a purpose-built overlay UI hid all token mechanics behind a single 'slide the estimate higher/lower' interaction. The transferable pattern for ceo.futarchy.fi is: lead with implied metric values and their delta, name tokens by what they pay ('VALUE-if-OUT'), and put the condition/called-off rule in a persistent plain-language alert.
dives.mechanism: MARKET TYPES: (1) Categorical & multi-categorical (Yes/No/... + automatic 'Invalid' failsafe outcome on every market). (2) Scalar: DOWN and UP tokens over [lowerBound, upperBound] stored as 1e18 fixed-point; at/below min DOWN redeems 1.0, at/above max UP redeems 1.0, linear in between; unit conventions live in the question text as a bracket suffix ('[USD per share]', '[gwei]', '[seats]'). (3) Multi-scalar: one templated question with a placeholder ('How many seats will [party name] win...') spawning N scalar outcomes — used for Dutch elections (6 outcomes) and Vitalik's deepfunding juror-weight round (60+ outcomes). (4) Conditional: 'New conditional market' can be attached to any outcome of a parent; the child's collateral IS the parent outcome token (mint/merge/redeem in parent tokens, not sDAI); nestable multiple levels; if the parent resolves to a different outcome, child tokens redeem for zero. (5) Futarchy: FutarchyFactory.createProposal(marketName, collateralToken1, collateralToken2, minBond, openingTime) deploys a FutarchyProposal; splitting each collateral via FutarchyRouter yields four tokens (e.g. YES_GNO/NO_GNO/YES_sDAI/NO_sDAI); TWO pools (YES_GNO-YES_sDAI and NO_GNO-NO_sDAI) let YES and NO tokens trade directly against each other instead of against cash; proposal passes if the YES pool's implied exchange rate exceeds the NO pool's; 'Invalid' auto-fails the proposal; winners redeem the underlying collaterals. TRADING: no bespoke AMM and no LMSR — ERC1155 CTF positions are wrapped to ERC20 (Wrapped1155Factory) and trade on Swapr (Algebra V3) on Gnosis or Uniswap v3 on Ethereum/Base/Optimism, quoted off Quoter contracts, with min-received slippage protection and a UI guard 'you cannot spend more than 1 sDAI to buy 1 outcome token'. Liquidity is permissionless concentrated-liquidity LPing (the app hands you off to Swapr's own interface). COLLATERAL/CHAINS: sDAI on Gnosis (yield-bearing — 'removes the cost of capital' for long-dated markets), DAI/sDAI on Ethereum, sUSDS on Base/Optimism. ORACLE: reality.eth 3.0 — question encoded at creation, answerable after openingTime; anyone posts an answer with a bond (min bond per market, e.g. 10 xDAI), challengers must at least double the bond, ~3.5-day timeout (302400s); correct answerers win the incorrect bonds; Kleros Court is the arbitrator of last resort (cross-chain arbitration proxies for non-Gnosis chains). CURATION: Kleros Curate 'Verified Markets' registry — refundable deposit + market/outcome images gets a 'Verified' badge; unverified markets show a warning. INCENTIVES: SEER token pre-launch emissions (~200M/month) to LPs, outcome-token holders, and Proof-of-Humanity-verified users (PoH boost); the API exposes a per-market 'incentive' weight (e.g. 333,333 SEER on a flagship market) used to steer liquidity to chosen markets; Foresight also seeded $25-50 trading credits to PoH/POAP holders (113 traders resulted). ADOPTION REALITY: futarchy contracts are live but documented under 'Futarchy (Test)' and every on-chain FutarchyProposal found via their API is an unfunded 'test-N'; their real decision-market wins were conditional markets (conditional KOSPI markets on President Yoon's fate — the impeachment-conditional forecast of 2443 matched actuals exactly; the Foresight movie-rating experiment where the market beat Criticker's model, MAE 32 vs 38).
ui_observations: Market cards lead with per-outcome odds as percentages (API 'odds': [51.1, 48.9, null]) plus liquidityUSD, openInterestUSD, category tags, chain, and a market image — images exist only because Kleros Curate verification requires submitting market+outcome images, so the verified list doubles as the pretty list.

Scalar outcomes are named literally 'DOWN' / 'UP' (+ hidden 'Invalid'), with the measurement unit crammed into the question title as a bracket suffix: 'What will be the price in USD of $TSLA by March 31st 2026? [USD per share]', bounds 200-600 as 1e18 fixed-point. The unit-in-brackets convention is also load-bearing for multi-scalar templating ('How many seats will [party name] win...').

Because odds are read straight from independent DEX pools with no normalization, thin markets display incoherent probabilities — a live deportations scalar showed DOWN 42.2% + UP 89.2% = 131%; markets with no pool show null odds and a hasLiquidity=false flag rather than fake 50/50.

Conditional markets get a persistent alert on BOTH the creation form and the market page stating the dependency, plus a blunt documented warning: 'If the parent market resolves to an outcome different from the one assumed by the child market... your outcome tokens will redeem for zero.' Each outcome of any market carries a 'New conditional market' link, making the condition tree user-extensible.

Trade panel: outcome list on the left, collateral amount + 'Buy' on the right, confirmation popup showing fees and minimum-received; a hard guard errors with 'you cannot spend more than 1 sDAI to buy 1 outcome token' so no one can overpay past certain-loss territory.

Resolution UX is outsourced: an 'Answer on Reality.eth!' button deep-links to reality.eth's interface for bond posting/escalation; report answer, raise dispute, and resolve market are separate documented steps rather than app-native flows.

Liquidity provision is also outsourced — 'Add liquidity' redirects to Swapr's concentrated-liquidity interface where the LP must pick a price range or 'Full Range' and hold both tokens; a real onboarding cliff and a context switch out of the product.

The Kleros Foresight experiment (foresight.kleros.io) is a dedicated simplified front-end skinned over Seer markets: 'rather than expose UP/DOWN token mechanics, you simply use a slider to predict higher or lower than the current market estimate'; one sDAI deposit auto-splits across all 16 movie markets; ratings were normalized to a 0-100 percentile scale to make the scalar band intuitive; Beginner Guide and Advanced Guide sit alongside the app; post-experiment they added resolved-value vs market-estimate shown side-by-side on each conditional market.

Futarchy creation flow is minimal: input proposal title, pick the two collateral tokens, set opening date, preview, 'Create Market' — but futarchy market pages expose four raw outcome tokens (Yes-sDAI/No-sDAI/Yes-GNO/No-GNO) with per-token odds, which is exactly the cognitive load that likely kept usage at zero.

Everything is queryable via a public serverless API (app.seer.pm/.netlify/functions/): markets-search with filters/sort/pagination, get-market by slug, markets-charts (hourly price/volume), get-portfolio — plus human-readable slug URLs per market; this made markets embeddable and analyzable (and made this study possible).
Onboarding docs are chain-concrete: separate GitBook pages for 'Deposit DAI on Ethereum' vs 'Deposit xDAI on Gnosis' etc., an llms.txt index, and .md suffix on every docs URL for machine consumption.
lessons: STEAL the Foresight overlay pattern: their scalar markets only became usable when a purpose-built front-end hid UP/DOWN tokens behind 'slide the estimate higher or lower than the current market estimate'. For ceo.futarchy.fi, make the primary interaction two big implied-value dials — 'Company value if CEO leaves: $X' / 'if CEO stays: $Y' — with the fire premium as the headline delta, and let a trade be expressed as dragging one dial ('I think value-if-out is higher than the market says'); demote OUT/STAY token prices to an Advanced tab.

ADOPT self-describing token names. Seer's futarchy tokens are condition+asset compounds (YES_GNO, NO_wstETH) and conditional docs say tokens 'redeem for parent outcome tokens'. Our OUT/STAY/EXIT triple is strictly worse: rename to condition-explicit compounds like VALUE-if-OUT, VALUE-if-STAY, and CEO-LEAVES so the symbol alone says what it pays and under which world.

ADOPT the persistent conditional-alert pattern, but invert its tone: Seer must warn 'if the parent resolves differently your tokens redeem for zero'; our called-off design refunds instead. Put a one-line banner on every conditional scalar: 'This market only counts if the CEO actually [leaves/stays] — otherwise trades are called off and you get your money back.' Seer proves users need this stated on the market page itself, not in docs.

ADOPT explicit scalar-band presentation: always show unit + [min,max] + the edge rule in one line on the trade panel ('settles 0 at or below $Xbn, 1 at or above $Ybn, linear between'). Seer's unit-in-brackets title convention ('[USD per share]') is a cheap, effective habit; ours should render band and unit as first-class UI fields, not title text.

AVOID their thin-market artifact and copy their gating: Seer displays unnormalized pool odds that can sum to 131% on thin markets, and shows null odds when there's no pool. Our LMSR can't produce incoherent sums (an advantage — say so), but adopt their hasLiquidity gating: never render a probability or implied value without liquidity behind it; show an explicit 'no market yet — be the first' empty state.

EVIDENCE for our architecture choice: Seer's pure futarchy product (four tokens, two pools, pair-vs-pair pricing) has zero funded markets, while their conditional decision markets with human-readable metric estimates (KOSPI-if-Yoon-outcome, movie percentiles) got real usage and even beat a personalized ML baseline. Presentation of conditional-metric-estimates > exotic mechanism purity; our two called-off scalars are the right shape, but the UI must lead with the side-by-side comparison the way Foresight's post-experiment UI shows 'resolved value vs market estimate' side-by-side.

STEAL percentile/normalized framing where absolute bands are unintuitive: Foresight predicted percentile scores 0-100 rather than raw ratings 'because they normalize ratings across different scales'. For CEO markets, consider displaying the scalar as % of the price band or as premium/discount vs current price, not just raw token price in [0,1].

ADOPT a resolution-transparency block: every Seer market carries the exact encoded oracle question, opening time, bond ladder ('challengers must at least double the bond'), arbitrator, and an always-present Invalid failsafe outcome with defined semantics (futarchy: Invalid = proposal fails). Our Sepolia markets should show an equivalent 'How this resolves' card: exact departure definition (what counts as the CEO leaving — fired vs resigned vs death), data source for the settlement price, timeline, and the called-off/refund rule.

STEAL yield-bearing collateral for mainnet: sDAI collateral 'removes the cost of capital' — critical for CEO-tenure markets that can run for years; on Sepolia it's moot, but bake the assumption into the design now.

STEAL demand-side bootstrapping tactics: per-market incentive weights (their API exposes 'incentive': 333,333 SEER on a flagship market) to concentrate rewards where they want price discovery, plus small sybil-resistant trading credits ($25 to PoH/POAP holders produced 113 traders in Foresight). A points program aimed at the flagship company pairs would complement our LMSR subsidy on the supply side.

ADOPT a public read API + slug URLs early: get-market/markets-search/markets-charts as plain JSON endpoints made Seer's markets shareable, embeddable, botable, and auditable; for a credibility-seeking futarchy product, one afternoon of serverless endpoints buys distribution and research usage.

AVOID outsourcing core flows the way Seer punts liquidity to Swapr and answering to reality.eth's own UI — each handoff is a context-switch cliff. Our single-contract subsidized LMSR already avoids this; keep trading, LPing (if any), and resolution status all in-app.

ADOPT two-tier docs: Seer ships a Beginner Guide vs Advanced Guide split (Foresight), chain-specific deposit walkthroughs, and an llms.txt + .md-suffix docs index for machine readability. For a mechanism as unusual as called-off conditional scalars, a 'first prediction in 3 clicks' beginner path separate from the mechanism whitepaper is the single cheapest clarity win.

dives.name: Seer documentation (GitBook) — seer-3.gitbook.io/seer-documentation
dives.summary: Seer is the Kleros-cooperative-built prediction market platform (app.seer.pm) whose GitBook is the ecosystem's best worked example of documenting conditional and scalar markets. The docs are ordered as a user journey (wallet → deposit → create → verify → mint/merge/redeem → buy/sell → report → dispute → resolve → LP → conditional → futarchy), backed by a developer section with sequence diagrams, viem code examples, full contract reference, subgraph queries, and an audit page. Key transferable content: scalar markets are taught as DOWN/UP tokens over a [min,max] band with a two-line worked payout example; conditional markets are taught as child markets collateralized by a parent outcome token, with a persistent UI alert and an explicit "redeems to zero if parent resolves otherwise" warning; futarchy markets auto-derive four token names (YES_GNO/NO_GNO/YES_wstETH/NO_wstETH) from the collateral pair and resolve "invalid = proposal fails." Resolution is Reality.eth escalating bonds with Kleros Court arbitration, all state exposed in the UI as buttons and statuses. Liquidity is plain DEX pools (Swapr/Uniswap V3) — no subsidized AMM — so thin markets simply block buys, a weakness ceo.futarchy.fi's subsidized LMSR already beats.
dives.mechanism: Market types: categorical, multi-categorical, scalar (exactly 2 tradeable outcomes DOWN/UP + an always-present Invalid outcome token), multi-scalar (one Reality.eth question per outcome via a bracket-placeholder template, payouts proportional to each answer, capped), conditional (CreateMarketParams.parentMarket + parentOutcome; the child's collateral is the parent's wrapped outcome ERC20, nestable arbitrarily deep; redeem returns parent tokens, or one-tx to base collateral via ConditionalRouter), and futarchy (FutarchyFactory, contracts labeled "test", Gnosis-only: two collateral tokens → four outcome tokens named YES_/NO_ of each symbol; two pools YES_A/YES_B and NO_A/NO_B; proposal passes iff YES-pair price > NO-pair price; "No invalid tokens: a proposal... can either pass or fail" — invalid resolves as proposal failed). Tokens: Gnosis Conditional Tokens Framework ERC1155 wrapped to per-outcome ERC20s (Wrapped1155Factory); mint/split 1 collateral → 1 full set, merge back, redeem after resolve. AMM: no in-house AMM or LMSR — outcome tokens trade on external concentrated-liquidity DEXes against the chain collateral: Swapr (Algebra V3) on Gnosis vs sDAI, Uniswap V3 on Ethereum (DAI), Base and Optimism (sUSDS); quoter contracts for pricing, exactInputSingle/exactOutputSingle with minimum-received revert protection; hard price ceiling "the maximum price at which an outcome token can be traded is 1 sDAI per token"; LP fees only inside the chosen range. Collateral is yield-bearing (sDAI/sUSDS) — "removing the cost of capital for traders." Oracle: Reality.eth per-market question (openingTime, timeout, minBond); escalating bonds ("you have to pay at least twice as much (bond) as the previous answerer"); correct answerers win prior wrong bonds. Dispute: fixed Kleros arbitration fee escalates to Kleros Court (cross-chain arbitration proxies where needed); ~14 days 12 h, +11 days per appeal; market stops accepting answers once arbitration is requested. Scalar resolution (RealityProxy.resolveScalarMarket): answer ≤ lowerBound → DOWN pays 1; ≥ upperBound → UP pays 1; in-band → UP = (value−min)/(max−min), DOWN = (max−value)/(max−min); invalid answer → Invalid token pays 1. Bounds stored in wei (1e18). Market curation: optional Kleros Curate "Verified Markets" registry — refundable xDAI deposit, policy criteria, Verified tag + images in UI. Incentives: SEER liquidity mining, boosted rewards for Proof of Humanity registrants, airdrops to Kleros jurors. Audits: Hats Finance competition on the core module with linked fix PRs.
ui_observations: Scalar teaching device: DOWN/UP tokens over a [min,max] band, always with a tiny worked numeric example — 'What will be the inflation in the eurozone in 2024? with a range of [0,10]%... If the inflation is of 2%, sDAI if Inflation DOWN tokens redeem for 0.8 sDAI each and sDAI if Inflation UP tokens redeem for 0.2 sDAI'. Token names embed collateral + condition + direction ('sDAI if Inflation DOWN'), so the payoff is readable from the name alone.

Conditional markets are created by a 'New conditional market' link attached to each outcome row of the parent market, and both the creation form and the resulting market page carry a persistent alert 'indicating that this is a conditional market, which depends on the outcome of another market' — the dependency is never implicit.

The killer warning is stated bluntly and repeated: 'If the parent market resolves to an outcome different from the one assumed by the child market... your outcome tokens will redeem for zero.' Docs also spell out that conditional trading uses parent outcome tokens (not sDAI) as currency, and that redemption returns parent tokens redeemable one level up.

Futarchy markets auto-derive all four token names from the two collateral symbols (YES_GNO, NO_GNO, YES_wstETH, NO_wstETH), present them as exactly two pools ('yes pool' and 'no pool'), and state the decision rule as one price comparison: pass iff YES_GNO/YES_wstETH > NO_GNO/NO_wstETH. Safe default is explicit: 'If the market resolves as invalid, the proposal is automatically considered failed.'

Prices are collateral-denominated with an explicit ceiling (max 1 sDAI per token), which makes price≈probability legible; notably the docs never convert scalar UP-token price into an implied forecast value — a gap.

Trade panel walkthrough: click outcome (left panel) → enter collateral amount (right panel) → Buy → confirmation popup showing price and fees → Continue. Thin markets are handled by blocking: purchases fail with an error when liquidity is insufficient, plus a minimum-received amount that reverts on price spikes.

Oracle state is surfaced as UI affordances, not prose: an 'Answer on Reality.eth!' button showing the required bond (e.g. 0.1 DAI), escalating-bond rule explained inline, market status updates when arbitration is requested (no new answers accepted), and a 'Check the Case on Kleros Court' button exposing dispute stage and expected waiting time per stage.

Trust signal: markets verified on Kleros Curate get a 'Verified' tag plus market/outcome images; unverified markets render plain. Verification deposit is refundable, and creators can verify at creation or later from their market card.
Onboarding docs are chain-split and pedestrian on purpose: wallet-and-network page, then per-chain deposit guides (DAI/sDAI on Ethereum; xDAI/wxDAI/sDAI on Gnosis), before any market concepts appear.

Docs infrastructure worth copying: an llms.txt full index and markdown versions of every page by appending .md to the URL (agent/LLM-friendly); developer section has sequence diagrams (create market, split/merge/redeem, question-and-resolve), copy-paste viem snippets, deployed-address tables per chain, subgraph GraphQL query examples, and an audit-reports page linking fix PRs.

Multi-scalar question templating in the create form: 'you should place the outcome type in brackets' — e.g. 'How many electoral votes will the [party name] win in the 2024 U.S. Presidential Election?' — one template stamps out N structurally identical Reality questions.
