LP flow deep-links from an 'Add liquidity' button on the market page to Swapr concentrated-liquidity position creation, with 'Full Range' offered as the safe choice for users unsure about ranges; fees accrue only inside the active range.
lessons: STEAL: value-anchored token naming. Replace bare OUT/STAY/EXIT with Seer-style '<payout> if <condition>' names derived mechanically from the structure — e.g. 'VALUE-UP if CEO-OUT', 'VALUE-DOWN if CEO-STAYS', and the binary as 'CEO leaves by <date>: YES/NO'. Seer's YES_GNO/NO_GNO and 'sDAI if Inflation DOWN' prove users can parse conditional scalars when the name encodes condition x direction.

STEAL: the two-line worked payout example on every scalar market page. 'If the company settles at $X within band [$A,$B], UP pays (X−A)/(B−A) and DOWN pays (B−X)/(B−A); at $2.5B in a [$1B,$4B] band, UP pays 0.50.' Seer's inflation example (2% → DOWN 0.8 / UP 0.2) is their single clearest device; hard-render it with the market's real band and current price.

STEAL: the persistent conditional-alert banner. Put a non-dismissable strip on each conditional scalar: 'This market only pays out if the CEO leaves [stays]. If not, it is called off and your collateral is refunded.' Seer repeats this on both creation and market pages. Bonus: our called-off-refund is friendlier than Seer's redeem-for-zero — advertise that difference explicitly, since it removes the scariest failure mode Seer has to warn about.

STEAL: render the parent→child tree. Seer creates conditionals from a link on the parent's outcome row, making lineage visible. Show each company as one card with the binary departure market as parent and the two conditional scalars as its branches (OUT branch → value market, STAY branch → value market), so 'fire premium' is visibly a difference between two children of one parent.

ADOPT: implied-value display (and beat Seer here). Seer never converts scalar token price to a forecast; we should headline 'market-implied company value if CEO out: $X.XB' = band_min + price_UP × (band_max − band_min), with raw token prices demoted to a details row. Likewise show the ranking table in implied-value and fire-premium terms, not token prices.

ADOPT: explicit resolution criteria + state machine in the UI. Seer exposes openingTime, bond size, escalation rule, dispute stage, and expected wait as buttons/statuses. Even with admin resolution on Sepolia, show: the exact resolution question text (what counts as 'CEO out' — fired vs resigned vs death vs interim), who resolves, when it opens, and current state (trading → observation → resolved/called-off). The EXIT ambiguity users report is precisely what Seer's per-market Reality question text + Verified-market policy solves.

ADOPT: a written market policy + 'Verified' style badge. Seer uses a Kleros Curate policy so every market's wording meets criteria. Publish one short 'CEO departure policy' document all markets share, link it from every card, and template the question like Seer's multi-scalar brackets ('Will [CEO name] cease to be CEO of [company] by [date]?') so all companies are uniform and the ranking is apples-to-apples.

ADOPT: safe-default resolution rule, stated up front. Seer futarchy: 'If the market resolves as invalid, the proposal is automatically considered failed.' Ours: state on-page what happens on ambiguity (e.g., ambiguous departure → STAY branch stands, OUT branch called off and refunded).

ADOPT: docs shape. Publish a small GitBook-style set ordered as a user journey (wallet/Sepolia faucet → what the three markets mean → trade → what happens at resolution), plus llms.txt and .md-suffixed pages so agents can ingest it; add a glossary defining OUT/STAY/EXIT/band/called-off in one place, and a developer page with contract addresses and a resolution sequence diagram.

KEEP/AVOID: keep subsidized LMSR — Seer's plain DEX-pool liquidity means thin markets just block buys with an error, and LP docs push range-management complexity onto users; guaranteed LMSR liquidity is a genuine advantage to advertise ('always tradeable, subsidized liquidity'). But steal their trade-confirmation popup (price + fee + minimum received) and the ≤1-collateral price framing that makes price read as probability.
LATER: yield-bearing collateral (sDAI/sUSDS) to remove capital cost for long-horizon CEO markets, and a refundable-deposit curation registry if market creation ever becomes permissionless.

CONTEXT: even the Kleros team ships futarchy as experimental — their futarchy contracts are labeled '(test)' and the feature is Gnosis-only with no liquidity-incentive docs; their production emphasis is categorical/scalar markets with strong oracle UX. The differentiated, mature part of Seer worth copying is explanation and resolution UX, not their AMM or futarchy implementation.

dives.name: Seer docs: Futarchy Markets (user guide) — plus app source deep-dive
dives.summary: Seer (built by the Kleros cooperative team) is a Gnosis-CTF prediction-market platform whose futarchy product is price-based paired conditional pools: one proposal contract mints 4 tokens (YES_GNO, NO_GNO, YES_wstETH, NO_wstETH) trading in two Swapr pools (yes pool, no pool); the social decision rule is 'accept if YES-pool price > NO-pool price', while on-chain resolution is a Reality.eth question ('Will proposal X be accepted by date Y?') arbitrated by Kleros Court, with the special rule that an Invalid resolution counts as proposal failed. Their strongest assets for us are scalar-market UX inventions found in the app source (seer-pm/demo): a 'Market Estimate' headline that converts UP/DOWN token prices into one number in real-world units, per-outcome tooltips spelling the exact piecewise redemption formula, a pre-trade 'forecast checker' payoff chart (enter your predicted final value, see your return), a 'Fill-to-estimate' order type (type your belief, the app computes the trades), and a one-sentence conditional-market warning banner. Their biggest weakness is the mirror of ours: the futarchy app UI never states the decision rule anywhere — a first-timer sees two 3-decimal price lines labeled Yes/No with no explanation of why higher-line-wins.
dives.mechanism: MARKET TYPES: categorical, multi-categorical, scalar, multi-scalar (payout = outcome answer / sum), nested conditional markets on any outcome, and futarchy. Built on Gnosis Conditional Tokens Framework; ERC1155 positions wrapped to ERC20 (Wrapped1155Factory) for DeFi composability. TRADING: no in-house AMM and no LMSR — outcome tokens trade on external DEX pools (Swapr Algebra concentrated liquidity on Gnosis; Bunni on mainnet), with optional CoW quotes; UI order types are Market, Fill-to-price (limit), Fill-to-estimate. Liquidity is permissionless LPing (LPs earn pool trading fees, concentrated ranges) plus Algebra FarmingCenter liquidity-mining incentives surfaced in-app and a SEER token airdrop program; no protocol trading fee documented. ORACLE: every market is a Reality.eth question (openingTime, timeout, minBond); answers posted with escalating bonds (each ≥2x previous, correct answerer takes prior wrong bonds); disputes escalate to Kleros Court for a fixed arbitration fee (~14d12h typical, +11d per appeal); RealityProxy writes payouts. Scalar payouts are piecewise linear over [lowerBound, upperBound]; regular markets include an explicit Invalid outcome token. FUTARCHY (Gnosis Chain only): FutarchyFactory.createProposal(marketName = Reality question 'Will proposal X be accepted by date Y?', collateralToken1/2, category, lang, minBond, openingTime) deploys a FutarchyProposal; 4 outcome tokens auto-named Yes-/No-<symbol>; two pools YES_T1/YES_T2 and NO_T1/NO_T2; collateral is the two project tokens themselves (e.g. GNO + wstETH), NOT stables — users split GNO into Yes-GNO+No-GNO then trade Yes-GNO↔Yes-wstETH inside a branch. Decision rule (advisory, off-chain): accept iff YES-pool price ratio > NO-pool price ratio. Resolution is on the DAO's actual action via Reality.eth; winning side redeems 1:1 for both underlyings, losing side → 0; futarchy markets have NO Invalid token — 'If the market resolves as invalid, the proposal is automatically considered failed' (invalid ⇒ NO wins), guaranteeing total settlement. VERIFICATION: separate Kleros Curate 'Verified Markets' registry — deposit xDAI bond, refunded if accepted; enforced against a published Verified Markets Policy / Market Resolution Policy. CHAINS: Gnosis (default; sDAI primary collateral, xDAI/wxDAI wrappers), Ethereum (DAI/sDAI), Optimism, Base; futarchy Gnosis-only.
ui_observations: Scalar bounds are welded to the outcome label: outcomes render as 'DOWN [30000,70000]' / 'UP [30000,70000]' with the range inline, plus a purple question-mark tooltip per outcome giving the exact 3-line piecewise formula: 'Redeem for (sDAI per token): Answer >= upper: 1 / Answer within [lower-upper]: (answer-lower)/(upper-lower) / Answer <= lower: 0' (Outcomes.tsx getTooltipContent).

Scalar markets get a single headline 'Market Estimate: <value> <unit>' banner — computed as DOWN_price*lowerBound + UP_price*upperBound from pool prices, with the unit parsed from a [unit] bracket convention in the market title (getMarketEstimate + getMarketUnit in SDK). Token prices are demoted; the human number leads.

Probabilities vs prices are type-dependent: categorical outcomes show normalized '%' odds (rescaled if they sum >100); scalar and multi-categorical show the raw token price 0-1 at 3 decimals; futarchy markets show NO odds at all (getMarketOdds returns NaN for type Futarchy).

Futarchy chart = two lines named just 'Yes' and 'No', each plotting the conditional pool's price ratio (1 YES_GNO in YES_wstETH terms, inverted from Algebra token0Price), 3-decimal precision, series sorted so the currently-higher line is first, with ECharts endLabel putting the series name at the right edge of the line; period tabs 1D/1W/1M/All. The decision is legible as 'which line is higher' — but no UI copy anywhere states that rule; and a Legend quirk appends '%' to futarchy price ratios, exactly the kind of unit confusion to avoid.

Pre-trade 'forecast checker' for scalars (ScalarForecastChecker.tsx): user enters their own forecast of the final value; UI shows a Buying UP / DOWN return-per-token table plus an ECharts payoff chart of potential return vs final value across the full [lower,upper] range (green UP line, red DOWN line, vertical mark line at the user's forecast), and highlights which side has the best return. Empty state: 'You need to buy more than 0 shares to calculate your potential return.'

'Fill-to-estimate' is a third order type next to Market and Fill-to-price: the user types a target market estimate (in market units, e.g. '2450') and a max collateral; the app computes a multi-leg trade plan that moves the market estimate to that number and shows the current estimate label. Users trade in belief-space, not token-space.

Conditional markets get a standing info banner (ConditionalMarketAlert): 'This market is conditional on the resolution of "<parent question>" being "<outcome>". If the main market resolves to a different outcome, the tokens of this market will be worthless.' — with deep links to the parent market and the specific parent outcome.

Futarchy market page layout groups by scenario, not token: exactly two selectable rows (Yes / No), each row a 2-column grid holding both tokens of that branch (Yes-GNO | Yes-wstETH), with per-row 'add liquidity' links and a radio to arm the trade panel; token names are always auto-derived (Yes-GNO), users never name outcome tokens.

Futarchy proposal creation is deliberately stripped to 2 steps (Outcomes -> Preview) vs the standard market's longer wizard: title, pick token + currency (four outcome tokens and two pools generated automatically), opening date, preview/deploy; docs show a screenshot per step.

Thin-market states are explicit: odds render 'NA' plus tooltip 'The odds cannot be displayed because the outcome's current price is far above 1... insufficient liquidity'; buys are disabled when you'd pay >1 collateral per share so buyers can't take 'a definite loss'; status chips distinguish 'Liquidity Required' from 'Trading Open'.

Lifecycle status chips use oracle-centric language: 'Reports not open yet' / 'Trading Open' / 'Liquidity Required' / 'Reports open' / 'Waiting for answer' / 'In Dispute' / 'Pending execution' / 'Closed'; winning outcomes get a green check icon after close.

Market list default ranking is 'Verification Status -> Liquidity' (tooltip says so), with alternate sorts Liquidity / Open Interest / Opening Date / Creation Date, and filters by 4 color-coded verification states (Verified green check, Verifying blue clock, Challenged orange law-balance, Not Verified purple exclamation). Verified markets carry images/avatars; unverified show a warning.

Each market card links every outcome token to the block explorer, shows your per-outcome balance, and a network icon reveals the proposal/market contract address — full on-chain legibility from the UI.

Resolution criteria are externalized into court-enforceable policy docs: the create flow links 'Verified Markets Policy' and 'Market Resolution Policy', and the docs walk through Report answer (bond amounts, 2x escalation) and Raise a dispute (fixed Kleros fee, 14d12h, +11d per appeal) with screenshots — the user is told exactly what happens when an answer is wrong.

Onboarding is chain-specific deposit guides (DAI/sDAI on Ethereum; xDAI/wxDAI/sDAI on Gnosis) plus an in-app DepositGuide and bridge widget; unsupported network shows 'Connect your wallet to a supported network' alert; multi-scalar titles use a '[party name]' placeholder-in-brackets convention so one template spawns many outcomes.
lessons: STEAL (highest value): the 'Market Estimate' headline. Convert each conditional scalar's token prices into one dollar number and lead with it: 'Market-implied company value if CEO leaves: $X / if CEO stays: $Y', computed exactly like Seer (down*lower + up*upper over the price band). Then define the fire premium visibly as the difference of the two estimates — the ranking number stops being magic.

STEAL: per-outcome redemption tooltip in Seer's exact 3-line piecewise style ('Settles at: price >= band top: 1; inside band: (price - low)/(high - low); <= band bottom: 0'), and weld the band to the label ('OUT-UP [$80, $160]'). Never show a scalar outcome name without its bounds.

STEAL: the pre-trade forecast checker. Before confirming any OUT/STAY trade, let the user type 'where do you think the share price ends if the CEO leaves?' and render the payoff line chart across the band with a mark line at their forecast plus a return-per-token table. This single widget does more against OUT/STAY confusion than any explainer text.

STEAL: 'Fill-to-estimate' as an order type. Let a visitor enter two numbers — value-if-out and value-if-stay — and have the app compute the LMSR trades that move both conditional estimates there (cap by max spend). Users express beliefs, not token mechanics; with subsidized LMSR this is even easier for us than for Seer's DEX pools.

STEAL: the conditional-market banner, but flip it to our advantage. Seer must warn 'tokens will be worthless' if the parent resolves the other way; our called-off design refunds. Put on every conditional market: 'This market only counts if <CEO name> actually leaves by <date>. If they stay, all trades here are called off and your money is returned.' One sentence, deep-linked to the EXIT market.

ADOPT: group the market page by scenario, not by token — Seer renders futarchy as two rows (Yes branch / No branch) each containing its pair, with a radio to arm trading. For us: two big scenario cards 'CEO LEAVES' and 'CEO STAYS', each containing its scalar market + estimate, and the EXIT binary shown separately as 'How likely is departure?' with a % — never mix the three as sibling outcomes in one list.

ADOPT: Seer's invalid-handling rule as explicit copy. Their 'if the market resolves invalid, the proposal is automatically considered failed' guarantees total settlement; publish our equivalent ('ambiguous/unresolvable departure counts as STAYS; OUT trades are called off') on every market so no branch can strand funds.

ADOPT: explicit thin-market states. 'NA' + tooltip when a price is unusable, buys disabled when they guarantee loss, and status chips separating 'Liquidity Required' from 'Trading Open'. On Sepolia with LMSR subsidy we can go further: show 'subsidized — always tradable' as a badge, a genuine advantage over Seer's empty-pool problem.

ADOPT: a short public 'Resolution Policy' page per market linked from the market header (what counts as departure: resignation/termination/not-renewed/death?, deadline, price band source and averaging window, tie/invalid rule), Seer-style, plus lifecycle status chips ('Trading Open' -> 'Waiting for answer' -> 'Resolved'). Seer can lean on Reality.eth+Kleros; we should at least name our oracle and dispute path with the same specificity (bond, timeout, who arbitrates).

AVOID Seer's gap: their futarchy UI never states the decision rule — two unlabeled-unit price lines and nothing saying 'higher line wins' (they even format futarchy price ratios with a spurious % sign). Print our rule as a sentence on the page: 'If the market values the company higher without the CEO, the market's verdict is FIRE' — and show the current verdict as a word, not just a premium number.

AVOID their trading friction, keep ours simple: Seer's futarchy requires splitting a volatile token into Yes/No legs before trading across two token-pair pools — powerful for token-price futarchy but heavy for newcomers. Our single-collateral subsidized LMSR against one contract is the better first-timer UX; don't copy the split/merge flow into the main path (keep it as an advanced tab like Seer's mint/merge).

ADOPT naming discipline: auto-derive all token names from the company/scenario (OUT-UP-ACME), never expose 4 raw token symbols in the primary flow, and use Seer's bracket-unit convention ('...company value [USD]?') so every number in the UI can carry its unit automatically.

ADOPT chart conventions: two-line OUT vs STAY conditional-value chart with series name as an end-label on the line (ECharts endLabel), higher series sorted first, 1D/1W/1M/All tabs, and the departure probability as a separate small % sparkline — don't overlay a probability and two prices on one axis.

CONSIDER later: a Kleros-Curate-style 'Verified' badge with color-coded states (verified/verifying/challenged) and default ranking by verification-then-liquidity; for CEO markets a badge certifying the resolution source (8-K filing, official announcement) would buy credibility cheaply once off Sepolia.

top_lessons: 1. (Foresight question phrasing + Seer Medium's 'if fired/if retained' verbs — copy-only, attacks our exact known weakness) Retitle every market as one plain conditional sentence and kill OUT/STAY/EXIT from the primary UI. Site change: company page headers become 'If [CEO] leaves [TICKER] by Sep 30, where does the stock settle?' / 'If [CEO] stays, where does it settle?' / 'Will [CEO] leave by Sep 30?'. Foresight ran an entire mainnet session where users never saw a token name; the 'If X, ...' prefix carried the whole conditional concept. Keep OUT/STAY/EXIT only as symbols in an Advanced/position view.

2. (Foresight's 'no profit, no loss' one-liner + Seer's persistent conditional-alert banner, inverted to our advantage) Put one non-dismissable sentence on every conditional card: 'This market only counts if the CEO actually leaves [stays]. Otherwise every trade is called off and your paid-in cash is refunded exactly — no profit, no loss.' Seer must warn 'tokens redeem for zero'; our escrowed-refund design is strictly friendlier, so this banner is both the clearest explanation of the mechanism and a selling point. Add post-resolution chips per card: green '(Resolved: $X)' / gray '(Called off — refunded)'. Copy-only.

3. (Seer's 'Market Estimate' headline + Seer's own futarchy-UI failure of never stating the decision rule) Lead every company card with two hero dollar numbers, not token prices: map each scalar's LMSR price through the band to dollars ('Market says $X if the CEO leaves / $Y if they stay') and show the fire premium as the delta between them. Then print the verdict as a word plus one sentence: 'FIRE — the market has priced the company higher without the CEO in ≥90% of the last week's snapshots' (our OldTek rule, currently invisible magic). Seer's futarchy pages show two unlabeled price lines and nowhere say 'higher line wins' — that gap is why their futarchy product got zero organic use. Frontend-only; the data already exists in our snapshots.

4. (Foresight's estimate-first slider / Seer's 'Fill-to-estimate' order type — the single proven fix for token confusion, 113 real mainnet traders never touched UP/DOWN) Replace the token buy form with a slider per conditional: the user drags to their predicted settle price against a pinned 'Market' estimate; the app computes the exact LMSR trade that moves the price there, capped at their budget. This is strictly easier for us than for them — cost to move an LMSR to target price p is closed-form, vs their Swapr tick-walking. Confirm popup shows '↑ Higher than the market' / '↓ Lower than the market' plus a plain receipt ('You bought $20 of if-out exposure at an implied $34.10; you profit if it settles above that'). Frontend + one view helper; no contract change.

5. (Foresight's dual-pin redemption slider + legend-as-leaderboard chart) Render each company as one price-band track with two labeled pins — 'if out: $X' and 'if stays: $Y' — so the fire premium is literally the visible gap; after settlement overlay a green 'Resolved' pin on the surviving branch and flip the called-off card to the gray chip. Make the ranking page a shared-axis fire-premium-over-time chart (one line per company, from our existing 30-min history.json) whose legend is sorted descending by current premium with search and hover-isolate — the legend IS the fire leaderboard. Our premium-as-%-of-spot is already the normalized cross-company number their percentile trick validates.

6. (Seer's resolution-transparency block + Kleros Curate's published-policy pattern) Add a 'How this resolves' card to every company page: exact departure definition (resigned / fired / announced-vs-effective / interim / kicked-upstairs / death), settlement source and timestamp (closing price 2026-10-30 21:00 UTC), the band [0.25×,1.75×]·spot with Seer's 3-line piecewise redemption tooltip welded to each scalar label ('≥ band top: pays 1 / inside: (value−low)/(high−low) / ≤ bottom: 0'), a stated ambiguity default (e.g. ambiguous departure ⇒ STAY stands, OUT called off and refunded), the 48h dispute window + oracle address, and a link to the 8-K/press-release standard. Plus one shared 'CEO departure policy' page linked from all 101 cards so the ranking is apples-to-apples. Copy + one component; huge credibility per unit effort.

7. (Seer's scenario-grouped futarchy page layout) Restructure the company page by scenario, not token: two cards 'CEO LEAVES' and 'CEO STAYS', each containing its implied-value estimate, band track, and slider; the EXIT binary rendered separately as 'How likely is departure? N%' with a small sparkline. Never list OUT/STAY/EXIT as three sibling outcomes in one table — mixing the probability market with the two value markets in one list is a large share of our confusion problem.

8. (Foresight's 4-slide first-visit modal + two-tier docs + direction-over-accuracy worked example) Ship a localStorage-gated onboarding modal: what this is (Hanson's textbook fire-the-CEO decision market, running live) → timeline (trade until Sep 30, settles on the Oct 30 close) → the called-off/refund rule → the behavioral P&L rule ('✅ you profit if your prediction moves the market price closer to the final value; ❌ you lose if it moves it away'). Split docs into a token-free beginner guide and an advanced 'what actually happens after your prediction' page containing the redemption math and their worked example that preempts the #1 novice complaint ('you predicted close to truth but the market was closer — you still lose'). Host as static pages — their Notion-hosted guides are uncrawlable, a documented mistake.

9. (Foresight's one-deposit-predicts-all + batch predict + CSV) Add a 'Predict all' flow: one pUSD approval, slider estimates across many companies, executed through a simple multicall router against our single contract (their per-user TradeExecutor smart wallet is only needed because they juggle external pools — a plain router suffices for us), skip-and-warn per failed market instead of failing the batch, CSV upload/download (row = company, P(exit), value-if-out, value-if-stays) for forecaster power users. Say the capital-efficiency line in the UI: one deposit can back estimates on every company. Medium effort, and it's what turned their niche experiment into 113 active traders.

10. (Foresight's calibration post-mortem + Session-1 selection-bias failure + honesty header) Commit now to the credibility loop: after the first cycle, publish a per-company table of resolved value vs final market estimate vs a named baseline (analyst consensus or an LLM), with average absolute error — their market-32-vs-Criticker-38-vs-ChatGPT table is what earned Session 2. State the company-pool rule prominently ('top-100 US by market cap — a neutral rule, no embattled-CEO curation') because their Session 1 ran 30+ points hot purely from pool-selection bias, and a curated fire-the-CEO list would inflate premiums the same way. Add the thin-market honesty header (countdown to Sep 30, unique traders, volume, and a per-market 'subsidized — always tradable' badge, the advantage their dry Swapr pools couldn't claim).
missed: Kleros Curate's actual Verified Markets Policy and Market Resolution Policy documents: named in five reports, never read. They are the court-enforceable wording standard for ambiguity and edge cases — the single best template for our 'what counts as departure' policy page. Follow-up: fetch the policy PDFs from the Curate registry.

Reality.eth v3 scalar mechanics: bond ladders and timeouts were described, but nobody dived into question templates and answer encoding for scalar/uint answers, min-bond sizing practice, the 'answered too soon' / event-occurs-before-openingTime rules, or Seer's RealityProxy adapter code. We need exactly this before wiring our swappable oracle to the Reality.eth v3 instance already on Sepolia (0xaf33…49CA).

Kleros Court dispute history on Seer/Foresight markets: 'Kleros Court as final arbitrator' appears everywhere, with zero data on actual disputes — frequency, cost, duration, overturn rate. A case-study dive on real Seer disputes would tell us what a contested 'did the CEO really leave?' (kicked-upstairs, co-CEO, medical leave) actually costs and takes.

The Yoon/KOSPI martial-law conditional markets: cited as Seer's best real decision-market result (impeachment-conditional forecast 2443 vs ~2443 actual) but never dived — band selection, liquidity depth, volume, trader count, and behavior under hour-by-hour crisis news. This is the closest existing analogue to a CEO-departure news cycle and deserves its own study.

The Vitalik/Gitcoin GG24 deepfunding round on Seer (60+-outcome multi-scalar juror-weight markets, ~$350k): named in passing as roadmap justification, not studied. It is a live, real-money futarchy-adjacent allocation deployment — the only Kleros-ecosystem instance where market output allegedly steered actual money besides Foresight's top-3 rule.

How Foresight measured the 'final market estimate' for the top-3 selection: unexplained. Snapshot vs time-weighted average, and any anti-manipulation window, directly bears on our OldTek 90%-of-trailing-week rule — worth confirming whether they got last-minute-manipulation attempts and how they'd resist them.

The 'no profit, no loss' neutrality claim was asserted, never verified across all trade paths: it holds for trade-wallet users holding full parent sets, but a user who bought one movie's UP directly from a pool with sDAI, or sold parent tokens mid-session, may not net to zero. Verify before citing Foresight as precedent — it likely shows our escrowed exact-refund is strictly stronger, which is a marketing claim worth substantiating.

CreditsManager / Foresight Credits contract design: the $25/$50 PoH/POAP program was described only at headline level — claim flow, sybil gating details, expiry, and whether credits distorted prices are unknown. Needed before any mainnet onboarding-credit program of ours.

SEER emissions steering: the per-market 'incentive' weight (e.g. 333,333 SEER on a flagship market) is named but the weight-setting process, PoH boost math, and whether emissions distorted market prices were not analyzed — relevant to whether we ever pair demand-side rewards with our supply-side LMSR subsidy.

Security surfaces referenced but not read: the kleros/futarchy-ui TradeExecutor contract (CREATE2 batch-multicall wallet) and Seer's Hats Finance audit findings with fix PRs. Read both before copying any batch-router/smart-wallet pattern, and mine the audit for CTF-wrapping bug classes.

Session 2 (movies-r2) backend retrofits — server-side P&L API, decongested chart, post-trade quick updates, email notifications — are listed as things they had to add after launch complaints, but the architecture wasn't dived; a cheap look at what they retrofitted is a checklist of what to ship day one.
synthesis.stack_verdict: Keep our contract stack, replace nothing, import their presentation layer, and augment only the oracle path. The evidence across all seven dives is unanimous that the Kleros/Seer trading layer is their weakness and our strength: outsourced CLAMM pools (Swapr/Uniswap) gave them fragmented liquidity, unnormalized odds summing to 131%, blocked buys on thin markets, and a tick-walking quote engine full of slippage patches — while their actual futarchy product (FutarchyFactory, four tokens, two pair-vs-pair pools) is still labeled 'test' and has zero funded markets on-chain. So: do NOT adopt Seer's AMM, the Gnosis CTF split/merge token plumbing, or their futarchy contracts. Our single subsidized LMSR contract with escrowed sell proceeds and exact refunds on the void branch is simpler, solvency-proven by our invariant suite, and strictly friendlier than their conditionals (theirs redeem to zero if the parent goes the other way; ours refund) — a difference we should advertise, not erase. What earned Kleros real traction was the market SHAPE we already have (called-off conditional scalars with a human-readable implied value — Yoon/KOSPI, Foresight) plus a purpose-built overlay UI that hides tokens behind belief-space interactions; that validates our architecture and says steal the UX, not the mechanism. The one genuine augmentation is resolution: short term (Sepolia), keep the operator oracle + 48h dispute window but publish Reality.eth-grade question specs per market — exact departure definition, opening/settlement times, and an explicit ambiguity default (their 'invalid ⇒ proposal fails' safe-default pattern; ours should be 'ambiguous ⇒ STAY stands, OUT called off and refunded') — which buys most of the credibility for free. Medium term, use our already-swappable oracle address to ship a Reality.eth v3 adapter (deployed on Sepolia at 0xaf33…49CA): bonded escalation is the battle-tested answer to the one hard problem we actually have — subjective departure edge cases (kicked upstairs, interim CEO, 'stepping back') — and it slots in without touching LMSR, refunds, or the fire-premium signal. Add Kleros Court (or any arbitrator) as final arbiter only once we're on a chain where it has jurors; on Sepolia it would be theater. Kleros Curate is worth copying as a pattern (published policy + verified badge), not as a dependency, and only if listing ever becomes permissionless.
